card.intuition: For every pair (i, j), check if routing through intermediate vertex k gives a shorter path. Iterate k from 0 to V-1 — after pass k, dist[i][j] holds the shortest path using only vertices 0..k as intermediates.
card.analogy: Building a phone directory by adding one relay city at a time: after considering all routes through city k, you know the best way between every pair of cities using relays from the first k cities.
when_to_use: All-pairs shortest paths on dense graphs where O(V³) is acceptable
Detecting negative cycles (dist[i][i] < 0 after the algorithm)
gotchas: Initialise dist[i][i] = 0, dist[i][j] = edge weight if edge exists, else ∞
The k loop must be the outermost loop — not i or j
For negative cycle detection: if dist[i][i] < 0 after the algorithm, node i is on a negative cycle

exercise.prompt: Implement Floyd-Warshall for all-pairs shortest paths.
hints: The relaxation step: if going i→k→j is shorter than the current dist[i][j], update it to dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]
exercise.test_code:
edges = [(0,1,3),(0,3,7),(1,0,8),(1,2,2),(2,0,5),(2,3,1),(3,0,2)]
dist = floyd_warshall(4, edges)
assert dist[0][0] == 0
assert dist[0][2] == 5   # 0->1->2
assert dist[0][3] == 6   # 0->1->2->3
assert dist[3][0] == 2   # 3->0 direct
assert dist[3][2] == 7   # 3->0->1->2
# Unreachable
edges2 = [(0,1,1)]
d2 = floyd_warshall(3, edges2)
assert d2[2][0] == float('inf')
print('PASS')
